summary: DHH argues that hybrid work arrangements represent the worst compromise between office and remote work. He contends that the push for hybrid is really a stealth reversal plot by managers who never liked remote work, starting with three days in-office before eventually eliminating remote days entirely. Hybrid destroys remote work's key benefits—geographic freedom, uninterrupted focus time, and wider hiring pools—while also undermining office work's advantages, since employees commute only to end up on video calls with absent colleagues. He advocates for a clear commitment to either fully remote or fully in-office work rather than a half-hearted middle ground.
key_points: Hybrid is a Machiavellian strategy by managers to gradually eliminate remote work while appearing to compromise
Hybrid destroys remote work benefits: employees must still live near the office, endure commutes, and lose uninterrupted focus time
Hybrid also undermines office benefits: employees commute in only to video-call colleagues who aren't there that day
The split between async and real-time communication creates a half-assed version of both information-sharing models
Companies should commit fully to either office or remote rather than choosing a cop-out middle ground
money_quotes: The honeymoon for remote work is over, and managers who never liked the concept to begin with are plotting its complete reversal.
This reversal plot is known as hybrid work, and combines the worst of in-office and remote work worlds.
By starting like this, managers who have no interest in remote work at all can claim to have "compromised", dulling the urge to revolt.
It also makes the interruption factory the norm once again.
A long commute can be crushing on the best of days, but it's twice as bad if it's in service of the same zoom fatigue you could have enjoyed from home.
When some of the information you need is written down and discussed online on the out-of-office days, and the rest lives only in the minds of people from oral tradition.
I say, do or do not. Hybrid is a cop-out.
key_insight: Hybrid work is not a genuine compromise but a strategic trojan horse that strips away the core advantages of both remote and office work, and companies should commit fully to one model or the other.
